title: Policy capacities and effective policy design: a review
Stories of how to give or take – towards a typology of social policy reform narratives
abstract: ABSTRACT Narrative stories are crucial to policy change, as they decisively contribute to how policy problems and policies are defined. While this seems to apply for social policy in particular, narrative stories have remained under-researched and not systematically compared for this area. In this article, we theorise on narratives in social policy by focusing on how similarities and differences between narratives in old- and new-social-risks policy reforms can be conceptualised, taking into account expansion and retrenchment. To systematically link those types of social policy reform with narrative elements, we rely on stories of control and helplessness, as well as the deservingness or undeservingness associated with different target populations. Thereby, distinct types of social policy reform narratives are identified: stories of giving-to-give, giving-to-shape, taking-to-take, taking-to-control, and taking-out-of-helplessness. The article concludes with empirical illustrations of those narrative types, which stem from the case studies presented in this Special Issue.

title: Performative policy: the case of Swedish healthcare reforms
abstract: In this article we analyze public sector change as a profoundly constructed phenomenon – as performative reforms. Public sector reforms, of which policy processes are an integral part, are constituted and realized through long chains of interventions. Communicative–discursive interventions posit and constitute problems as real and important, while technocratic interventions, such as plans, analyses, and schemes construct new imagined worlds for possible and attractive instrumental solutions. Our empirical results display circular movements of three modes of change, making up a continuous policy cycle in the transformation of Swedish health, reiterated on different levels of the system, in different scales, and with different actors involved. The continuity of the reforms is to a large extent the result of a successful institutionalization of the policy cycle and its content. It is stabilized as a set of discourse and social technologies, distributed throughout the entire healthcare system and almost impossible to question.

title: Policy Capacity Within the Local Anticorruption Political Agenda (A Study of Corruption Prevention in the Post-Covid-19 Economic Recovery Program in Malang City)
abstract: This research aims to assess the local government’s capacity to develop anti-corruption policies for social assistance programs (Bansos) and post-Covid-19 economic recovery. The study examines deeply the effectiveness of the policy framework in combatting corruption at the local level. The research findings are intended to form the keystone for crafting regional anti-corruption policy strategies, particularly focusing on preventing corruption in social assistance funds during the Covid-19 pandemic. The research location chosen is the Government of Malang City, given the occurrence of widespread corruption within the local government in 2018 (https://antikorupsi.org/id/article/korupsimassal- wakil-rakyat-daerah). Have there been systematic efforts to enhance corruption prevention? This research was conducted between 2021 and 2022 during the economic recovery period following the Covid-19 pandemic, utilizing document analysis, mass media reports, and Focus Group Discussions (FGD). Employing content analysis and qualitative methods, this study highlights the necessity for innovative approaches to strengthen corruption prevention and eradication policy capabilities. While there is a commitment from local governments, concrete programs and action plans are still needed. Moreover, stricter mechanisms and enhanced oversights are essential to bolster corruption prevention. Keywords: corruption prevention, local government policy, policy capacity, economic recovery

title: The Dynamics of Change
abstract: Abstract The ideas and discourse in agents’ discursive interactions appear at three levels of generality: at the immediate level of specific policies; at an intermediate level of more general programs, which add policy instruments, methods, and goals; and at the deepest level as philosophies, which constitute core ideas and discourse that underlie policies and programs. Policies, programs, and philosophies follow different dynamics of change (and continuity). The processes by which stand-alone policies take hold, succeed, or fail are affected by characteristics internal to the policies such as credibility and acceptability as well as by external factors related to viability, goodness of fit, and timing. The processes by which general programs change (or continue) encompass revolutionary transformations when instruments, methods, goals, and core ideas change all at once or evolutionary mechanisms of incremental change involving reinterpretation, recreation, or resilience. In deep philosophies, such processes may involve abrupt shifts, dialectical or recurring transformation, slow evolution, or continuity with little change.

title: List of Figures
Conclusion
abstract: Abstract This concluding chapter summarizes the arguments in the book and asks one final question. It first restates the definition as well as purpose of discursive institutionalism. It then reviews the different forms of ideas and discourse, the types of arguments, the kinds of discursive processes, and the two spheres of agents’ discursive interactions, along with their institutional contexts. It follows with the dynamics of change in policies, programs, and philosophies; the power of ideas and discourse—persuasive as well as coercive, structural, and institutional; and the construction of meaning and methods. It next covers the history of political science, along with the three other neo-institutionalisms in discursive institutionalist perspective, and then discusses the epistemological and ontological questions raised by all such approaches, as answered in the philosophies of science and social science. The chapter concludes with an answer to the question about what scholars whose work fits under the discursive institutionalist umbrella have in common.

title: Historical Origins and Development
abstract: Abstract Discursive institutionalism has its roots in the interpretive modes of explanation of the post-World War II period. While that period was characterized by methodological pluralism, with interpretive and historical methodological approaches holding their own against systemic and behavioral or rational choice approaches, the 1990s brought methodological “wars” among the newly minted “new institutionalist” frameworks for analysis. These pitted a dominant rational choice institutionalism against historical institutionalism and sociological institutionalism, while increasingly marginalizing scholars whose interpretive, ideational/discursive approaches fell under the umbrella of discursive institutionalism. Over time, however, discursive institutionalism has come to be accepted as an equally valid and, indeed, essential perspective on political reality. The chapter details this trajectory and then provides brief histories of each of four analytic frameworks—rational choice, historical, sociological, and discursive institutionalism.

title: Ideas and Discourse
abstract: Abstract This chapter outlines the great variety of ideas and discourse. It categorizes them according to three forms of ideas and discourse and two types of argument along with the three kinds of discursive processes through which they may be activated. As forms, ideas and discourse serve as (i) the evocative symbols and metaphors, myths and historical memories that people use to re-envision the past and influence the present; (ii) the integrative narratives and storytelling, frames and framing that people deploy to shape public perceptions, to build identity or to act as guideposts to knowledge; and (iii) the overarching public philosophies and worldviews people develop to create their big-picture orientations to the world or the ideologies and scripts that serve to structure people’s thoughts or actions. As types of arguments, all such ideas and discourse tend to be infused with cognitive arguments focused on knowledge and necessity and normative arguments concerned with values and appropriateness. As kinds of discursive processes, discursive exchange, discursive persuasion, and discursive domination may deploy any and all such forms and types of ideas and discourse in political settings.

title: Agents’ Discursive Interactions in Institutional Context
abstract: Abstract Ideas and discourse not only come in many different varieties but also appear in the discursive interactions through which agents coordinate the construction of ideas in the policy sphere and communicate about them in the political sphere. There are many different kinds of agents interacting in the two main institutional contexts of the public sphere. These include ideational policy entrepreneurs and discursive policy communities in the coordinative discourse of the policy sphere and ideational political entrepreneurs with informed publics and the general public in the communicative discourse of the political sphere. But institutional contexts are also more specific. In addition to being internal to agents, derived from their polity’s contexts of meaning and logics of communication, they are external to agents, as the formal institutions that structure discursive interactions. With regard to governmental organization, these can be seen as on a continuum from national “simple” polities, to “compound” polities, to supranational “highly compound” polities.

title: Preface
The Power of Ideas and Discourse
abstract: Abstract In addition to classical kinds of power (including coercive, structural, and institutional) are ideational and discursive power. Ideas and discourse don’t just matter; they have power. Ideational/discursive power encompasses agents’ persuasive power through ideas via discourse to convince others of their validity, agents’ coercive power over ideas and discourse to impose their views on others, and the structural and institutional power in agents’ ideas and discourse to embed ideas in societal rules or to structure thought through discourse. These different ideational/discursive kinds of power are operationalized through various mechanisms, including processes of identification through coalition magnets, frames of reference, and empty signifiers; diffusion through dissemination, transmission, emulation, and translation; reinvention through ideational bricolage and discursive repertoires; and learning through updating knowledge and beliefs.

title: The Power of Ideas and Discourse in Political Analysis
abstract: Abstract Ideas and discourse are essential to politics as well as to political analysis. In this masterful book, Vivien A. Schmidt provides a sweeping appraisal of the many different ways in which scholars in political science and adjacent fields explain how ideas and discourse are used by policymakers and everyday citizens to understand the world, to influence others, and to mobilize for collective action. In so doing, she brings together, for the first time in one place, the many facets of her highly original approach to the power of ideas and discourse, developed over the past quarter-century under the name of discursive institutionalism. Schmidt’s masterful book not only serves to constitute the very large “field” of ideational and discursive approaches falling under the umbrella of discursive institutionalism. It also, as in the subtitle-outlines her distinctive discursive institutionalist perspective, highlighting her philosophical premises and methodological preferences while delineating the boundaries with competing approaches, including rationalist, historical, and sociological institutionalism. Schmidt’s ultimate goal is to promote a methodological pluralism which recognizes that because political reality is complex, it requires a diversity of perspectives, with agents’ contextualized ideas and discursive interactions at its core.

title: Introduction to Discursive Institutionalism
abstract: Abstract Ideas and discourse have always been essential to our understanding and explanation of politics and society. And yet, beginning in the 1990s, mainstream political science paid comparatively little attention to them. This has changed over time, not only as politics has become increasingly unstable and unpredictable but also as scholars have developed a wide range of approaches concerned with the substantive content of agents’ ideas and their interactive processes of discourse in institutional context. Discursive institutionalism serves as an umbrella concept for all such approaches. It is an analytic framework that illuminates how agents construct meaning and exercise ideational and discursive power in ways that generate change (or continuity) in politics and society. This chapter provides an introductory overview of discursive institutionalism, including the varieties of agents’ ideas, discourse, and discursive processes; their discursive interactions in institutional contexts; the dynamics of change; the nature of power and legitimacy; the construction of meaning and methods; and the philosophical grounding of the approach. The chapter ends with a brief outline of the book.

title: Philosophical Underpinnings in the Philosophy of Social Science
abstract: Abstract Philosophers of social science have long divided between those who believe that the social sciences could emulate the natural sciences and those who argue that the subjects of social scientific explanation differ fundamentally from the objects of natural scientific explanation because of the nature of human beings as sentient or “reflexive” agents. Among the latter, further questions involve divisions over the nature of knowledge and certainty, with the attendant dangers of universalism or relativism, and divisions related to the nature of structure and agency, with the attendant risks of determinism or voluntarism. This chapter offers a way out of the quandaries of knowledge and certainty by differentiating among degrees of certainty for different kinds of knowledge (building on Wittgenstein) and of structure and agency by introducing the concepts of background ideational abilities (building on Searle) and foreground discursive abilities (building on Habermas).

title: Philosophical Underpinnings in the Philosophy of Science
abstract: Abstract The philosophy of science from the 1950s to the 1980s divided among four rival accounts of the nature and validity of scientific explanation. Although they focus on the model of physics, these different approaches mirror the four main explanatory methods of the social sciences—law-like, systemic, historical, and interpretive. Law-like approaches seek to discover universal truths or approximations thereof (e.g., Hempel, Nagel, and Popper); systemic approaches expect programmatic success (e.g., Kuhn, Lakatos, and Feyerabend); historical approaches look for progress over time (e.g., Toulmin and Laudan); and interpretive approaches privilege scientific creativity (e.g., Polanyi and Latour). The only thing on which these philosophers of science agreed is that they see little hope for the social sciences to be scientific in the manner of the natural sciences. Our question therefore is: why should political scientists have sought to emulate what philosophers of science said about what scientists (in physics) did when what philosophers of science did was to deploy the four methods of the social sciences to explain science? But many did, nevertheless.

title: New Institutionalisms
abstract: Abstract The three traditional neo-institutionalisms—rational choice, historical, sociological—differ as much from one another as they do from discursive institutionalism. The differences are apparent in their objects and logics of explanation, in their definitions of actors and institutions, in their explanations of and approaches to change, and in the problems they confront with regard to explanations of change (and continuity). They also differ in the extent to which they have turned to ideas and discourse, and how well they are able to accommodate discursive institutionalist approaches. The chapter examines each of the three neo-institutionalisms in discursive institutionalist perspective by considering their scope and limits, their ability to endogenize change, and their turn to ideas (and discourse).

title: List of Tables
Intellectual Trajectory
abstract: Abstract This chapter offers insights into the origins and development of discursive institutionalism through the lens of the author’s own intellectual trajectory. It moves from accounts of the author’s educational background and early career to her invention of discursive institutionalism in response to changes in political science, then on through the various phases of theoretical development of discursive institutionalism in tandem with its empirical applications to questions of democracy and legitimacy, capitalist political economy, and the welfare state. The chapter is elucidated not only by brief summaries of the author’s own scholarly contributions but also by anecdotes involving those contributions and/or responses to them.

title: The Construction of Meaning and Methods
abstract: Abstract In this chapter, we delve into my theoretical perspective on the construction of meaning, with knotty questions about how sentient agents make sense of their lives and the world. The chapter contrasts sentient agents’ ideas about their interests in response to reality, material and social, with rationalist assumptions about rational actors’ material interests. It differentiates rationalist scholars from scholars of ideas and discourse along a continuum from positivism to constructivism. It also distinguishes between kinds of constructivists and their methods, including those using more positivist quantitative methods. As for norms and values, it considers the normative bases of politics and the normative biases in political research. Advances in moral psychology and neuroscience also make clear that emotion and passion are inseparable from reason such that sentient agents are not just thinking and speaking but also feeling beings. All of this affects the ways people use cognitive and normative arguments in processes of legitimation in areas such as international human rights and liberal democracy.
